topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged on my phone, startling me mid-scroll through campus news. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My stomach clenched, a cold knot tightening beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, expecting a reminder about a late assignment, but the message was a photo—a picture of me, taken moments before I left the library, captioned simply: "Good studying, Alex." My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly. I reread the message three times, the fluorescent lights of the common area suddenly feeling too bright, too exposed. I quickly minimized the chat, shoving my phone deep into my bag, the fabric rough against my palm. Every sound in the room seemed amplified: the distant chatter, the hum of the ventilation system. I kept glancing toward the doorway, half-expecting him to materialize, a shadow lingering just beyond the frame.

[story 2]

It happened during my evening wind-down. I was logging into Instagram, intending only to check my feed, when a profile picture popped up: Mr. Harrison’s smiling face, but overlaid with a slightly blurred background that looked suspiciously like the campus quad. A sudden wave of heat washed over my cheeks, and I instinctively pulled my sweater tighter around myself. Why was he looking at my activity? I scrolled down, heart beginning a frantic rhythm against my ribs. Then I saw it: a story update. It was a short video clip of me struggling with a particularly dense physics problem, the caption reading, "Persistence pays off. Keep pushing, Alex." A slow, creeping feeling began to coil in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I closed the app abruptly, the screen feeling suddenly hot against my fingers, and focused intensely on the pattern of the carpet beneath my feet, trying to ground myself.

[story 3]

I found the evidence tucked away in an old folder on my laptop, a screenshot of a post on a private academic forum. It was Mr. Harrison. He had tagged me in a discussion about a recent lecture, a post that was entirely innocuous, yet it sent a jolt of alarm straight through me. My hands started shaking so badly I had to press them flat against the desk to stop the tremor. The air in the small study room felt thick, stagnant. I stared at the screen, the pixels sharp and accusatory. I tried to rationalize it—maybe it was a coincidence, a misinterpretation of a shared group. But the way his profile picture was positioned next to my name, the casual familiarity of the interaction, it all felt deeply wrong. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight, and waited, holding my breath, for whatever came next.
